item.text:
Verdict: do not commit this as P3-LR-STORE in its current form. The literature set is mostly real and contains useful material, but several load-bearing conclusions are unsupported or contradicted by the cited sources. It is a useful draft of the ledger’s categories, not a completed Phase-0 de-risking review.

## Main findings

1. The historical “maintenance killed these systems” thesis is not established.

The strongest problem is [STORE.md §4](/home/ec2-user/css/kernel/kernel-of-truth/docs/next/lit/STORE.md:201):

- XCON: the cited AAAI paper confirms 6,200 rules, approximately 50% annual change, and serious maintainability problems—but says runtime performance was satisfactory and DEC was building a more maintainable successor. The contemporaneous CACM account describes XCON as the successful cornerstone of a growing production family; it reports about 40%, not 50%, annual rule churn. Neither establishes that maintenance cost “killed” XCON, exceeded its value, or was fundamental rather than an implementation/representation problem. [AAAI XCON-in-RIME paper](https://cdn.aaai.org/AAAI/1987/AAAI87-148.pdf), [Barker and O’Connor text](https://www.thefreelibrary.com/Expert%2Bsystems%2Bfor%2Bconfiguration%2Bat%2BDigital%3A%2BXCON%2Band%2Bbeyond.-a07100854)
- Freebase: the cited report says Google wound down the standalone service and transferred data to Wikidata. Google’s migration paper attributes the decision to Wikidata’s success—not to an economic finding that Freebase was “unsustainable” or “not worth its maintenance cost.” Those claims appear invented. [Google migration paper](https://research.google/pubs/from-freebase-to-wikidata-the-great-migration/), [contemporaneous shutdown report](https://searchengineland.com/google-close-freebase-helped-feed-knowledge-graph-211103)
- Cyc: the person-century/~1M axioms figure is genuine, but “the bottleneck was fundamental” and “no matched-cost win exists after 40 years” are absence-of-evidence conclusions from a very small, non-systematic sweep. Lenat’s paper supplies effort, not that causal verdict. [Lenat 1995](https://papers.baulab.info/papers/knowledge/Lenat-1995.pdf)

Consequently, the cross-system conclusion that maintenance/acquisition economics was “the historical killer” and makes K-P3v2(6) load-bearing is overstated. The literature supports “maintenance is a material risk requiring explicit accounting,” not “this is the usual demonstrated cause of failure.”

2. The economic denominator is technically wrong, and the claimed store “win” is unmatched.

At [lines 83–88](/home/ec2-user/css/kernel/kernel-of-truth/docs/next/lit/STORE.md:83) and again [lines 290–293](/home/ec2-user/css/kernel/kernel-of-truth/docs/next/lit/STORE.md:290), the review says cost per checkable record is at least the $219 cost per curated article. That does not follow. If an article yields \(n\) accepted facts, its allocated curation cost is approximately \(219/n\), plus fact-level normalization, review and maintenance costs. It can be below or above $219. The unknown article→accepted-record yield is precisely what must be measured.

Accordingly:

- $0.078/legal generated record and $219/curated article do not form a valid bracket.
- $1.75/abstract, $219/article and person-years/axiom are not a coherent “per-record range.”
- Cyc’s figure is per axiom, contradicting the claim that every figure is per source item.
- Rejected candidates, multi-fact articles, duplicate facts and record-type complexity are absent from the denominator.

The external-update claim at [lines 139–150](/home/ec2-user/css/kernel/kernel-of-truth/docs/next/lit/STORE.md:139) is also too strong:

- Replacing a keyed record may be average-case O(1), but detecting the change, checking provenance, finding dependent assertions, propagating entailments, revalidating consistency, rebuilding indices and rolling back are not generally O(1) or local.
- RippleEdits is evidence of exactly such ripple effects; it does not measure symbolic-store lifecycle cost.
- External context is not a uniquely neural-symbolic advantage. Conventional RAG, search/tool-use and in-context systems get the same separation between model weights and mutable knowledge.
- Therefore “real, defensible, matched-accounting advantage” is premature. The comparison is against ROME/MEMIT, not against the strongest neural retrieval/search baseline required by the GPT-5.6 review. [RippleEdits](https://aclanthology.org/2024.tacl-1.16/), [large-scale model editing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2401.07453)

The sound design statement is narrower: externalized knowledge makes individual source records directly replaceable and inspectable; whether this lowers end-to-end update TCO must be measured against matched external-context baselines.

3. Phase-1 remains substantially un-de-risked.

The review usefully identifies broad ledger categories, but [§7](/home/ec2-user/css/kernel/kernel-of-truth/docs/next/lit/STORE.md:312) leaves open nearly every parameter that would determine a P3-D-LIFE or P3-D-SEAL decision:

- cost per accepted, checkable record;
- candidate rejection/attrition cost;
- review quality/cost operating point;
- provenance cost;
- change-arrival distribution and refresh cadence;
- dependency-aware revalidation cost;
- fraction of useful facts reachable through the mapper;
- staleness rates;
- competitive-coverage scale.

That is category discovery, not a cost model. P3-D-LIFE still needs a quality-adjusted denominator, fixed versus variable costs, record classes, author/reviewer time distributions, rejected-candidate accounting, dependency fan-out, refresh/reindex/rollback costs, and uncertainty ranges.

FreshQA also does less for P3-D-SEAL than claimed. It shows that evaluated LLMs struggle with rapidly changing questions and that search augmentation helps. It does not prove that facts “go stale continuously,” estimate a per-fact refresh rate or cost, or require a curated persistent store rather than live search. [FreshLLMs/FreshQA](https://aclanthology.org/2024.findings-acl.813/)

P3-D-SEAL must additionally decide whose cost is being charged: benchmark-producer truth refresh, participant-authored store maintenance, or both. Producer-side fresh-fact construction should not be charged asymmetrically to the store system while a neural/search baseline receives the evaluation evidence or live search for free.

## Factual and citation errors

- [STORE.sources.jsonl entry 2](/home/ec2-user/css/kernel/kernel-of-truth/docs/next/lit/STORE.sources.jsonl:2) gives the wrong authors. DOI baw115 is by Hirschman, Fort, Boué, Kyrpides, Islamaj Doğan and Cohen—not Good and Su.
- The same entry conflates two experiments. The $1.75 drug-indication experiment obtained 96% precision and 89% recall with five annotations. The ~90% precision/~70% recall number describes cited expert performance, not that crowd result. The separate gene-mutation crowd experiment achieved roughly 80% precision and >70% recall for under $1/abstract. [Full OUP article](https://academic.oup.com/database/article/doi/10.1093/database/baw115/2630501)
- Barker and O’Connor report about 40% annual rule change; the 50% figure is in the Soloway XCON-in-RIME paper. [sources entry 7](/home/ec2-user/css/kernel/kernel-of-truth/docs/next/lit/STORE.sources.jsonl:7) attributes the combined figures incorrectly.
- [Freebase entry 10](/home/ec2-user/css/kernel/kernel-of-truth/docs/next/lit/STORE.sources.jsonl:10) claims its source cites “unsustainability.” The linked article contains no such stated rationale.
- The Freebase migration figures are real: 100+ users, ~90,000 approval/rejection actions and >14M uploaded candidates. But that is not a measured labour cost, and only a small fraction of candidates had received an action by January 2016. Calling provenance verification “the bottleneck” is an inference, not a paper result. [Primary migration PDF](https://research.google.com/pubs/archive/44818.pdf)
- UniProt’s 35–50% capture figure is accurate, but “saturates,” “fixed corpus,” and “hard human ceiling” are stronger than the paper. UniProt explicitly prioritizes particular hard-to-predict functional data; the number alone does not establish an immutable saturation ceiling. [UniProt 2019](https://academic.oup.com/nar/article/47/D1/D506/5160987)
- The Wikidata quality paper supports three quality indicators, but its abstract does not establish staleness economics or continuous refresh cost. [Wikidata quality study](https://arxiv.org/abs/2107.00156)
- “15 verified at their primary venue” in [the epistemic register](/home/ec2-user/css/kernel/kernel-of-truth/docs/next/lit/STORE.md:381) is misleading. DOI/title existence is bibliographic verification, not claim-level verification. Several exact claims came from snippets or secondary quotations, and at least two are materially misstated.
- No source appears wholly fabricated; the problem is inaccurate metadata, conflated results, and causal conclusions not present in the sources.

## Ranked top three concerns

1. The XCON/Freebase/Cyc causal history is presented as the review’s load-bearing verdict but is not supported; XCON and Freebase are materially mischaracterized.
2. The cost-per-record conversion is mathematically invalid, while the O(1) “matched-accounting advantage” compares against a weak parametric-editing straw baseline rather than matched RAG/search/external-context systems.
3. The hand-off overstates closure: it identifies cost buckets but leaves the quantities, operating points and measurement protocol that determine Phase-1 decisions almost entirely open.

Commit decision: not sound to commit as the final P3-LR-STORE deliverable. It is sound to retain as a draft after correcting the citations, downgrading the historical causal claims, removing the invalid $219 lower bound, and reframing Phase-1 as an empirical measurement design rather than a largely de-risked implementation hand-off.
